title: Routing-Aware Sparse Mixture-of-Experts for Latency-Bounded Inference
abstract: We present RA-MoE, a sparse mixture-of-experts architecture in which router logits are biased toward experts whose recent activation history minimizes inter-device communication. On a 47B-parameter language model with 32 experts and top-2 routing, RA-MoE reduces p99 decode latency by 38% relative to standard learned routing while keeping perplexity within 0.4% of the baseline. The mechanism requires no extra parameters and uses an exponential moving average of expert utilization. We further show that the routing entropy regularizer common in prior MoE work harms latency, and propose a stability-only auxiliary loss that recovers throughput without sacrificing quality.

title: Hierarchical Attention for Million-Token Document Retrieval
abstract: Standard dense retrievers degrade on documents longer than their training context. We propose HA-Retrieve, a two-stage encoder that first scores fixed-size sentence windows with a small encoder, then routes the top-32 windows through a larger cross-encoder that produces a single document embedding. On a corpus of long scientific reports averaging 480K tokens, HA-Retrieve improves recall@10 by 17 points over a flat BGE-large baseline and reduces index size by 6x. Latency at query time is held constant by precomputing window embeddings offline. We release a 50K-document benchmark of long-form legal and biomedical text.

title: Polyglot-Switch: A Benchmark for Intra-Utterance Code-Switching in Open LLMs
abstract: We introduce Polyglot-Switch, a 12K-utterance benchmark covering nine language pairs in which speakers switch between languages within a single sentence. Evaluating 11 open-weight models from 1B to 70B parameters, we find that perplexity at the language-switch boundary is on average 4.2x higher than on monolingual spans, with the gap widest for Tamil-English and narrowest for Spanish-English. Switch-token accuracy correlates more strongly with byte-pair-encoding vocabulary overlap than with model size. We release evaluation scripts and a synthesized training set derived from bilingual movie subtitles.

title: Draft-Verify Speculative Decoding with Adaptive Draft Length
abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by drafting K tokens with a small model and verifying them in parallel with the target model. We observe that the optimal K is highly input-dependent: factual prompts tolerate K=8 while code completions require K=2 to avoid wasted verification. AdaSpec dynamically tunes K per request by tracking a running estimate of the draft acceptance rate. On Llama-3 70B target with a 1B draft, AdaSpec yields a 2.7x end-to-end speedup over fixed-K=4, with no measurable quality regression on HumanEval, MT-Bench, or GSM8K.

title: Tokenizer Equity: Measuring and Mitigating Length Disparities Across 104 Languages
abstract: Subword tokenizers trained on English-heavy corpora produce 2-4x more tokens per character for low-resource languages, inflating both training cost and inference latency. We quantify this Tokenizer Equity Index across 104 languages for six widely deployed tokenizers and find that GPT-4o and Llama-3 each have at least 18 languages with a TEI below 0.4. We propose a vocabulary patching procedure that adds 8K targeted merges without retraining the model, recovering 92% of the latency gap on Swahili, Telugu, and Burmese. The patch is shipped as a drop-in JSON file consumable by the tokenizers library.

title: Distilling Chain-of-Thought Reasoning into 1B-Parameter Students
abstract: We study whether explicit reasoning traces from large teachers can be distilled into 1B-parameter student models. Naive supervised fine-tuning on teacher traces overfits to surface patterns and underperforms on held-out reasoning categories. Our approach, Trace-Critic Distillation, filters teacher traces with a learned critic that scores logical consistency before they enter the student's training set. On a 12-task reasoning suite (GSM8K, BBH, ARC, etc.), 1B students trained with Trace-Critic Distillation match the average accuracy of an 8B baseline at 9% of the inference cost, while a vanilla-distilled control trails by 11 points.

title: Reranker-Free Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Listwise Embedding Alignment
abstract: Modern RAG pipelines bolt a cross-encoder reranker onto a bi-encoder retriever, doubling query latency. We instead fine-tune the bi-encoder with a listwise loss that distills the reranker's ordering signal directly into the embedding space, removing the reranker at inference. On HotpotQA and BEIR, our Listwise-Aligned BGE matches reranked baselines on nDCG@10 within 0.6 points while halving query latency. Crucially, the technique requires only the reranker's scores on the existing training set, not new annotations. We release training scripts and aligned checkpoints for three open embedding models.

title: Steering Vectors for Robust Instruction Following Under Prompt Injection
abstract: We extract activation steering vectors that increase a model's adherence to its system prompt in the presence of injected adversarial user instructions. The vectors are computed as the mean difference of residual stream activations between clean and injected prompts on a held-out probe set. Applied at inference, they reduce successful injection rate on a 400-attack benchmark from 41% to 7% on Mistral-7B-Instruct without retraining and with under 0.3% impact on MMLU. The defense is composable with prompt-level defenses and does not require access to the attacker's distribution.

title: Bootstrapping Low-Resource Translation with LLM-Generated Pivot Data
abstract: For language pairs with fewer than 50K parallel sentences, we use an instruction-tuned 13B LLM to generate synthetic translations through a high-resource pivot (English). A round-trip consistency filter removes 38% of generations as unfaithful. Training a 600M-parameter NMT model on the filtered synthetic set plus available bitext improves BLEU on FLORES-200 by an average of 6.3 points across 22 low-resource languages, with the largest gains on Lao, Tigrinya, and Quechua. We document failure modes including hallucinated named entities and propose entity-anchoring at inference.

title: Constitutional Critique Loops with Verifiable Reward Signals
abstract: Constitutional AI improves model behavior by iterating critique-and-revise loops against a written constitution, but the critique step itself can be miscalibrated. We introduce Verifiable Constitutional Critique (VCC), where each constitutional clause is paired with a programmatic verifier (e.g., regex, SQL, unit test) wherever possible. The verifier provides a deterministic reward that anchors the LLM critique. Across 200 hand-written constitutions for tool-use agents, VCC improves clause adherence by 23 points and reduces critique-revision cycles needed for convergence from 4.1 to 1.6 on average. We open-source a library of 60 reusable verifiers.
